text: Lithium dendrite growth in lithium ion and lithium rechargeable batteries has been observed to be a serious issue in relation to battery safety. In order to mitigate this effect it is essential to understand the mechanism of lithium dendrite growth in the battery during operation. In situ 7Li magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was conducted in situ on the electrolyte and lithium metal electrodes of a symmetric lithium cell allowing us to understand and relate the behavior of the electrolyte concentration gradient in the space between the electrodes and the types of structures and their growth rates on the Li metal electrode that correlate to safe battery operation. We use chemical shift imaging of the metal electrodes as a very sensitive diagnostic tool in order to understand clearly the difference types of features formed on the electrode surface and to identify any features related to dendrite growth between the electrodes. Our CS imaging experiments reveal that mossy structures on the anode are formed from the beginning of charge on the anode of every cell we examined; while the formation of dendrites occurs at much later time. We develop simple metrics to analyze the MR data set and to compare different charge regimes, applied in a series of identical cells charged at different current densities. We show that high current charge regime behavior of dendrite formation time is strongly correlated to that of electrolyte depletion at the electrode surface both from experiment and from theory based on the Sand's time model, while at low currents we see the development of a second dendrite growth mechanism that is apparently independent of the electrolyte concentration in the bulk liquid electrolyte.
